I was never a particularly happy person. I was never quite able to shake the feeling that something was missing from my life, no matter how hard I tried. I tried to fill the emptiness with friends, with hobbies, with anything that I could think of, but nothing ever seemed to work. The feeling only got worse as I got older, until I reached the point where I could barely function. I became depressed and withdrawn, and my family was at a loss as to what to do.

I started to think about suicide. It seemed like the only way to escape the pain that I was feeling. I couldn't see any other way out. I didn't want to burden my family with my problems, so I decided that the best thing to do would be to kill myself.

I was about to go through with it when something stopped me. I don't know what it was, but something inside me said that suicide was not the answer. I realized that I would be leaving my family behind to deal with the pain and the guilt, and I just couldn't do that to them. So I decided to seek help instead.

input:
The backstory:

I rolled in my mom's car in the desert on May 23rd, 2014. Me, my friend and mother were all involved.

Luckily, I got the worst of the aesthetic injuries, but my mother broke her neck and had a stroke and such. She made a full recovery and is walking/talking to this day. My friend walked away with cuts and bruises. Everything in the car (electronics, clothes, laptops) survived, except the car itself. I'm extremely lucky that I walked away with an almost missing arm only. Don't know what caused the split though, whether it was just pressure from the car on top of it, or if it was a shard of glass tearing through.

I was life flighted to Vegas where I got stitched up. My muscle had torn all the way through (that's all tendon and ligaments), and that had to be sutured, my skin was stapled back together. I got a skin graft about 2 months later and now it's almost fully healed.

More images of the following months [here in this album](<URL>

Rolling in a car is definitely an unreal experience. I constantly dream of the night that it happened, the rolling playing over in my head again and again. Luckily, a doctor and nurse were some of the first to stop and help. Definitely thankful for everyone who helped me.

Oh, and by the way, life flights cost about $44,000 in case you were all wondering. That and all my other bills amounted to ~$150,000 that night. Phew!

input:
I've been self-harming for about six years now. It started as a way to cope with the pain I was feeling after my parents died. I withdrew from my friends and stopped participating in the things I used to enjoy. I felt like I was just going through the motions of life and I didn't see the point in anything anymore.

Around that time, I started seeing a therapist and she suggested I try some coping mechanisms other than self-harm. I tried a few things, but nothing seemed to work. I still felt empty and like I was just going through the motions.

Then, one day, a friend of mine suggested I try playing the online game League of Legends. I had never played anything like it before, but I decided to give it a try. At first, I was terrible and got frustrated easily. But I kept playing and slowly started to get better.

I found myself getting lost in the game and forgetting about my pain. It was a way to escape from my reality and into a world where I could be anyone I wanted to be. I slowly started to re-engage with my friends and even made some new ones.

League of Legends gave me something to look forward to and a reason

input:
One of the hardest things to deal with is depression. It's like a dark cloud that follows you around everywhere you go. And it's especially hard to deal with when you're a child. But there are things you can do to cope with it. 

One of the best ways to cope with depression is to find healthy ways to express yourself. This could be through writing, painting, drawing, or even just talking to someone you trust. It's important to find an outlet for your feelings so that you don't internalize them and end up harming yourself.

If you're feeling particularly depressed, it's also important to reach out for help. This could be from a friend, family member, therapist, or even a hotline. Remember that you are not alone and there are people who care about you and want to help.

The girl sits in the therapist's office, silent and withdrawn. She has been coming here for months, and the therapist has learned that it is best to let her talk when she's ready.

Finally, the girl speaks. "I just don't see the point anymore," she says quietly. "Everyone would be better off without me."

The therapist nods, understanding the girl's feelings but also knowing that they are not true. "Can you tell me more about why you feel that way?" she asks.

"I'm just so tired," the girl replies. "Tired of fighting, tired of being alone. I just want it all to stop."

The therapist gently probes further, trying to get the girl to open up about what is really going on. She asks about her home life, her friends, her interests. Slowly, the girl begins to talk, and the therapist starts to get a better understanding of her situation.

Eventually, the girl begins to talk about her worries for the future, and the therapist begins to see the signs of depression. She encourages the girl to keep talking, and together they begin to develop a plan to get her the help she needs.

I think it is unethical to eat your own species. Also it carries the risk of contracting Prion diseases like CJD and Kuru, though if you avoid eating the brain you should be good. I don't think evidence of past infanticide and cannibalism justifies doing it currently. 

However, I see morality as a spectrum rather than a binary thing. If you are starving to death, you might aswell eat the baby as it won't survive on its own and you can just have another when resources are plentiful. Eating a human would be less ethical/more morally wrong than a working class animal, which would be more unethical than eating any random animal, which would be more unethical that eating a plant, which would be more unethical than just eating the fruit of the plant and dispersing the seeds, which could be considered a net positive.

I think we can attribute the usefulness of a species to society as a contributer to its value. Ie, if you had to chose who to live, it would be morally better to pick a doctor over a murderer.

As for humans being of negative value. I think that damage we do is part of a cycle every intelligent species goes through on every inhabited planet, and a filter. If we don't smarten up and stop warring and destroying our environment, we won't survive. I think all our negative traits can be explained by evolution (greed, tribalism) and we need to overcome them to succeed. We are the only chance that Earth based life gets off the planet, weather it is synthetic biology, or from us seeding life elsewhere in the universe through terraforming (unless microbial life has already seeded life elsewhere). I think that everything living is made up of universe and exists within the universe, and is in a literal sense, the universe experiencing itself. A universe devoid of life is a sad thought. Therfore, more life, especially intelligent life is a good thing. Considering it took 4.6 billion years for life to evolve humans, and the sun only has about 4 billion years left (though it will prob make life on Earth impossible before then, maybe 2 billion years?), we are prob Earth based life's only chance to get out into the universe. 

I think nature is far more cruel than any human could be and that we have the possibility to lower the net amount of suffering in the universe. Though humans are part of nature too. One example of nature's cruelty would be cats. They kill things just for the fun of it and as far as we can tell, are incapable of empathy. By a humans standards (which it is unfair to judge a cat by), they would all be considered narcissistic psychopaths. Humans are capable of deciding to kill animals more ethically, while some animals like bears and hyenas will eat their prey while still alive and squirming. In this sense, it may even be reasonable to say humans are (one of) the only moral creature on the planet as we tend to empathize with the animals we kill and eat (at least in most cultures). Infact, we empathize with our prey so much, many humans become vegetarian by choice to lower tha amount of damage and suffering we cause (not me though).

input:
Frankly, speaking as a feminist, I'm mostly in agreement with you. Reproductive rights are important across the board, and men should have a fair deal of choice when it comes to it.

However, I still want to introduce some nuance here.

* There's a pragmatic concern here related to the welfare of the child. We don't want mothers and children living in poverty, and that has historically been a widespread problem that carries other social ills. Child support was invented as a measure not to say "punish" men, but rather to ensure that children were at least fed, clothed, housed and educated to prevent the suffering and social problems associated to lacking these things in early childhood. 

* As a corollary, I would require the introduction of robust child welfare programs to guarantee that, absent child support, the child will still have chances to do well and the mother won't fall into poverty. This includes things like free childcare programs, food assistance, and programs not unlike the Finnish baby boxes. I would still make these available to couples raising children together, though.

* Like you said, it is important that all parental rights be abdicated in cases that the father wishes to abstain from child support. It is also important that the father be "duly notified" of the pregnancy. I think the mother would have to demonstrate clear attempts to contact and notify the father within some specified timeframe. 

* Like you also kind of said, in many ways, raising a child is more onerous than a pay cut, in that it involves a lot of unpaid, thankless labor. This in itself creates an asymmetrical relationship between the burdens placed on each parent, and this in turn has to be considered when trying cases related to "financial abportion". This is why forcing women to "take responsibility" for an accident is placing a much greater burden on them than child support would be. <Person> broadly, I agree with you that options for men should exist, but you are obscuring the asymmetrical nature of the issue.

Questions:

 Treatment of a SAH can include surgery, embolization, medications, and a watchful waiting. What factors are considered in determining treatment? Factors to be considered include the size of the bleed, type and location of aneurysm, and the overall health of the patient. 

 



 A subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) is a type of stroke caused by bleeding in the space around the brain. This space is called the subarachnoid space, and it contains the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) that surrounds and cushions the brain.

The most common cause of a SAH is the rupture of an aneurysm. Aneurysms are weak spots in the blood vessels that supply the brain. They are usually caused by a combination of genetic and environmental factors, and they can occur without any warning signs.

Other causes of SAH include trauma to the head, bleeding disorders, and use of certain medications.

The symptoms of a SAH can vary depending on the amount of bleeding and the location of the bleed. They may include a sudden, severe headache; nausea and vomiting; stiff neck; blurred vision; loss of consciousness; seizures; and paralysis.

A SAH is a medical emergency, and it requires immediate treatment. The goal of treatment is to stop the bleeding and prevent further damage to the brain.

The most common treatment for a SAH is surgery. The type of surgery will depend on the location and size of the aneurysm. In some cases, the aneurysm can be repaired with a coil or clip. In other cases, the aneurysm <DateTime> need to be removed.

Other treatments for a SAH include embolization and medications. Embolization is a procedure in which a small device is inserted into the aneurysm to prevent it from rupturing. Medications can be used to reduce the risk of rebleeding and to help control the symptoms.

In some cases, a watchful waiting approach may be used. This is usually only an option for people who are not experiencing any symptoms.

The decision about which treatment to use will be made by a team of doctors. Factors that will be considered include the size of the bleed, the type and location of the aneurysm, and the overall health of the patient.

Title: Negotiating Across Cultures
[ARTICLE START]

Issues of Culture in Negotiation

Negotiation is the process by which two or more parties attempt to reach an agreement. The goal of negotiation is not necessarily to reach an agreement, but to improve the never-obtainable conditions for an agreement, that is, it reduces uncertainty and risk . Negotiation occurs in a variety of places such as commerce (buying and selling), politics, between individuals, etc.

Negotiation is not just an agreement to complete a task together, but the building of social capital . The investment in relationships with others builds trust. Trust and cooperation are essential to creating "win-win" situations for all parties involved. Therefore it is necessary that negotiations be taken seriously and performed appropriately (Figure 1).

One of the most important aspects to a negotiation is that it includes some element of disagreement. When disagreements are present, the possibility of an adversarial relationship between parties becomes more likely. In negotiations, usually one side prefers a collaborative strategy while another party prefers a competitive strategy. The competitive nature of American society makes the U.S. a very competitive place to do business. To <PersonType>, competition is viewed as being "clean, fair, and square" in which both parties compete openly for the same prize. Furthermore, they feel that it is their duty to win this competition .

Suture and Staple Removal

Patients usually see the family or general practitioner for suture removal following laceration repair from an injury. In many offices, it is the medical assistant’s responsibility to remove the sutures or staples. It is vital to check the emergency center’s report regarding the number of sutures or staples put in so you can be sure to remove all of them.
        Be sure to remove the same number of sutures or staples as were inserted by the provider. Sutures or staples that are not removed can become infected, so care in removing all the material is vital. Occasionally patients report that one or two stitches have already come out during a bandage change at home. This should also be noted in the patient’s chart. Follow the steps in Procedure 48–4 for suture or staple removal. Removal of staples is performed with a staple extractor as described in the procedure for removal. Before you remove the sutures or staples, check the number of days the provider who put them in recommended as the time to wait before removal. Ask the provider to inspect the healing wound. After the sutures or staples have been removed, providers might order additional closure materials to cover healing incisions or lacerations (Figures 48–4A, B, C, and D). A support skin closure might be necessary to keep the skin together until the wound is completely healed. The type of supportive closure should be noted in the patient’s chart.

Just imagine a relationship based on lies, deceit and dishonesty,
sounds disgusting right? That’s how it is when you haven’t built
trust in your partner.

The happiest, most satisfying relationships rest on a foundation of
implicit trust. If you want your relationship to be all it can be, both
of you must learn how to create this kind of trust. No matter the
type of relationship, building trust is essential to creating a
sustainable and lasting bond with another human being.

Most people are afraid of revealing their true inner sides in love. At
first, the guy and the girl are scared to kiss, and then they’re
scared to go to second base, and then to the third. Once they get
past that, life gets a bit manageable and both of you start to open
up with each other.

Once you know how to build trust in a relationship, the relationship
enters the next little stage and gets much happier and relaxed.
Take the time to understand what your partner is looking for in a
relationship and make sure your needs and feelings are expressed.
When you begin there, building trust becomes much easier. To help

in your partner”, I will write on some ways that will definitely help
